content.problemStatement:
Suzumo is the apprentice of Chef Goro. Today, there is a big contest in CodeChef. The contest has JJJ judges, so they have to prepare JJJ dishes, one for each judge.

Before preparing the dishes, Suzumo collects all the ingredients; for simplicity, the ingredients are labeled 111 through MMM.

Chef Goro has many hands. Each of his hands can only take all ingredients from a specific subset; also, if one of his hands can take all ingredients from a set AAA and another hand can take all ingredients from a set BBB, then he has a hand which can take all ingredients from their intersection A∩BA \cap BA∩B. Moreover, it is known that Goro has a hand which can take all the ingredients (it exists, even if it's not listed in input).

Goro has so many hands that it is inconvenient to describe all of them in the input. Therefore, you are only given information on NNN of his hands. Goro said that using that information, it is possible to generate all of his remaining hands. For each iii (1≤i≤N1 \le i \le N1≤i≤N), let's denote the set of ingredients taken by the iii-th hand by SiS_iSi​.

To prepare the iii-th dish (for each 1≤i≤J1 \le i \le J1≤i≤J), Goro uses a recipe that consists of a set of ingredients RiR_iRi​. Suzumo is in charge of preparing the base of the recipe, which is the set of all ingredients xxx (not necessarily present in the recipe) such that no matter which hand Goro uses to take xxx, he will end up also taking another ingredient (different from xxx) that belongs to RiR_iRi​.

Write a program to help Suzumo find the size of the base of each recipe.
constraints: each hexadecimal string contains only the following characters: digits '0' through '9' and lowercase letters 'a' through 'f'
each decoded set SiS_iSi​ contains only numbers ≤M
each decoded set RiR_iRi​ contains only numbers ≤M
the sum of NNN over all test cases is at most 10^5
the sum of JJJ over all test cases is at most 10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows. The first line of each test case contains three space-separated integers MMM, NNN and JJJ. For each valid iii, the iii-th of the following NNN lines contains a single hexadecimal string encoding the set SiS_iSi​ taken by the iii-th of Goro's hands. For each valid iii, the iii-th of the following JJJ lines contains a single hexadecimal string encoding the recipe RiR_iRi​.
content.outputFormat: For each recipe, print a single line containing one integer — the number of ingredients in the base of the recipe.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example case 1: Goro has the following hands: {1},{1,3,4},{1,2,3,4},{1,2,5},{1,2},{1,2,3,4,5}. Note that the hand {1,2} is not provided, but it can be generated as the intersection of {1,2,3,4} and {1,2,5}. In the first recipe, the base of {2,5} is {5}. The hands that can take ingredient 5 are the 4-th and 6-th hand, and both of them also take ingredient 2, which belongs to the recipe. In the second recipe {1,2,3}, the base is {2,3,4,5}. Ingredient 1 is not in the base, because the first hand can take only ingredient 1 without taking another ingredient from the recipe.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we first need to decode the given hexadecimal strings into sets of integers. Then, for each recipe, we need to find the base of the recipe, which consists of all ingredients xxx such that no matter which hand Goro uses to take xxx, he will end up also taking another ingredient (different from xxx) that belongs to the recipe. We can achieve this by iterating over all possible hands and checking if the hand can take the ingredient xxx and another ingredient from the recipe.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * (N * M + J * M)) — where T is the number of test cases, N is the number of hands, J is the number of recipes, and M is the maximum number of ingredients.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N * M + J * M) — for storing the decoded sets and the recipes.
